title: Containers running Node.js (Express) apps freeze when using Rosetta on macOS with linux/amd64 platform
body:
### Issue Description

### Bug description

I'm experiencing an issue when running containers built for linux/amd64 on macOS. The containers run Express-based Node.js applications. When started with Rosetta enabled, the containers eventually freeze and stop serving new HTTP requests. However, switching off Rosetta and using QEMU-based emulation allows the containers to run stably without freezing.

Environment:
- Host OS: macOS (Apple Silicon)
- Podman version: [5.4]
- macOS version: [14.6.1 (Sonoma)]
- Node.js version: [22.11.0]
- Container base image: [debian:bookworm-slim]
- Container build platform: linux/amd64

Steps to reproduce
1.  Enable Rosetta in Podman and restart podman machine.
2. Build a container image for linux/amd64 that runs an Express.js app.
3. Start the container and schedule parallel HTTP requests per second to the Express app.

Observe: After some time, the container stops responding to new requests.

Expected behavior:
The container should continue running normally and serve requests indefinitely.

Actual behavior:
The container hangs after some time, and no new requests are served. No crash logs or errors are printed inside the container.

Actual behavior:
The container hangs after some time, and no new requests are served. No crash logs or errors are printed inside the container.

Workaround:
Disabling Rosetta and running the container with QEMU.

Additional notes:

The issue does not seem to be reproducible with lightweight services or non-Node.js apps.

Could be related to Rosetta thread scheduling or networking emulation in combination with Node.js event loop behavior.

### Operating system

macOs 14.16.1 Sonoma

### Installation Method

None

### Version

1.19.1

### Steps to reproduce
1.  Enable Rosetta in Podman and restart podman machine.
2. Build a container image for linux/amd64 that runs an Express.js app.
3. Start the container and schedule parallel HTTP requests per second to the Express app.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Additional context

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1.  Enable Rosetta in Podman and restart podman machine.
2. Build a container image for linux/amd64 that runs an Express.js app.
3. Start the container and schedule parallel HTTP requests per second to the Express app.


### Describe the results you received

The container hangs after some time, and no new requests are served. No crash logs or errors are printed inside the container.

### Describe the results you expected

The container should continue running normally and serve requests indefinitely.

comments.body:
First you are using an older macos and podman version so your first step should be to update everything to the latest version and see if this changes anything. 

But unfortunately I don't see what podman can do about this we either ship rosetta and qemu, if rosetta doesn't work with your application then you need to figure out why exactly and report this to apple instead and hope they fix rosetta.

Besides this problem there is the bigger problem that rosetta is generally broken on newer linux kernels so we will disable rosetta with 5.6 https://blog.podman.io/2025/06/podman-and-apple-rosetta/ which then would make this work again out of the box by using qemu instead which you said works. However we may in the future enable rosetta as default beack because it seems to be much faster (if it works)

> First you are using an older macos and podman version so your first step should be to update everything to the latest version and see if this changes anything.
> 
> But unfortunately I don't see what podman can do about this we either ship rosetta and qemu, if rosetta doesn't work with your application then you need to figure out why exactly and report this to apple instead and hope they fix rosetta.
> 
> Besides this problem there is the bigger problem that rosetta is generally broken on newer linux kernels so we will disable rosetta with 5.6 https://blog.podman.io/2025/06/podman-and-apple-rosetta/ which then would make this work again out of the box by using qemu instead which you said works. However we may in the future enable rosetta as default beack because it seems to be much faster (if it works)

Just to clarify - We already tested this with multiple machines and with updated macos and different versions of podman. No luck!
Additionally, it works pretty good in Docker with rosetta enabled.

I know docker uses a custom kernel so maybe they have some patches or maybe some other kernel config options that avoid this. I don't know where these patches are for someone to have a look. Anyway building our own kernel would not be something we can handle.

Other than that check for other differences memory/cpu,etc... maybe something around networking.

Also you haven't really provided a reproducer either.

> Build a container image for linux/amd64 that runs an Express.js app.

That can mean anything really, reproducer needs to be exact so there is no guess involved. Ideally just provide a minimal Containerfile and podman commands to build and run then maybe someone can try to reproduce.

Here is the minimum reproducer. I created a Docker image for https://github.com/bezkoder/node-express-mongodb and ran MongoDB on the same network using Podman. Below are the steps I followed. 1. Pull the code from https://github.com/bezkoder/node-express-mongodb and modify the MongoDB URL in `node-express-mongodb/app/config/db.config.js` to `mongodb://mongo:27017/bezkoder_db`. 2. Create a Podman network named test-network using: `podman network create test-network` 3. Run the MongoDB container using: `podman run -d --name mongo --network test-network mongo:4.4 mongod --bind_ip_all --port 27017` 4. Build the app image using: `podman build -t mongo-app-image:latest -f Dockerfile .`. Use the following Dockerfile to build the app image: ``` FROM node:18-alpine WORKDIR /node-express-mongodb COPY . . RUN npm install EXPOSE 8080 CMD ["node", "server.js"] ``` 5. Run the app using: `podman run -d --name mongo-app --network test-network -p 8080:8080 mongo-app-image:latest` 6. Scheduled postman calls to `http://localhost:8080/api/tutorials` and `http://localhost:8080/api/tutorials/published` with 50 virtual users as shown below. Observed : Within 30 sec to 1 min, container stopped responding.

I'm not sure what to do there.  We support our main branch here and in the main branch, rosetta is disabled which @Luap99 mentioned.  So to me this is fixed in main.  That said, maybe you could poke around and see if you can tell why it froze?

If you ssh into the machine, can you access any meaningful logs? Are the processes in Linux alive or ?  You could install strace if so and see if you figure out whats going on.  Also maybe check the journal?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
